audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed as continuation of the whirlwind response: wild goats, deer, wild donkey, wild ox, ostrich, horse, hawk, and eagle. Emphasis on untamed creaturely wisdom and divine care beyond human control.

verses.restored: Do you know the time when the wild goats of the rock give birth? Do you watch the labor of the deer?
Can you number the months they fulfill, and do you know the time they give birth?
They bow down, bring forth their young, and send out their labor pains.
Their young become strong; they grow up in the open; they go out and do not return to them.
Who sent out the wild donkey free, and who loosed the bonds of the swift donkey,
to whom I gave the desert as his house and the salt land as his dwelling?
He laughs at the noise of the city; he does not hear the shouts of the driver.
He searches the mountains as his pasture and seeks after every green thing.
Will the wild ox be willing to serve you? Will he spend the night at your manger?
Can you bind the wild ox in the furrow with ropes, or will he harrow the valleys after you?
Will you trust him because his strength is great, or leave your labor to him?
Will you trust him to return your seed and gather it to your threshing floor?
The wings of the ostrich beat joyfully, but are they the pinions and feathers of covenant-kindness?
For she leaves her eggs on the earth and warms them in the dust,
and forgets that a foot may crush them, or a wild animal trample them.
She treats her young harshly, as though they were not hers; her labor is in vain, without fear,
because the Divine made her forget wisdom and did not share understanding with her.
When she lifts herself on high, she laughs at horse and rider.
Do you give the horse strength? Do you clothe his neck with a mane?
Do you make him leap like the locust? His majestic snorting is terror.
He paws in the valley and rejoices in strength; he goes out to meet weapons.
He laughs at fear and is not dismayed; he does not turn back from the sword.
Upon him the quiver rattles, the flashing spear and javelin.
With trembling and rage he swallows the ground; he does not stand still when the horn sounds.
At the horn he says, Aha! From afar he smells the battle, the thunder of commanders and the shouting.
Is it by your understanding that the hawk soars and spreads its wings toward the south?
Is it at your mouth that the eagle mounts up and makes its nest on high?
On the rock he dwells and lodges, on the tooth of the rock and the stronghold.
From there he searches for food; his eyes see it from far away.
His young ones lap up blood, and where the slain are, there he is.
